metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback rule when auth/gateway controls are absent from the diff (e.g., 'downgrade to WARN with an explanatory note; only BLOCK when exposure is definitively in the diff and no auth pattern is present anywhere in the changed files').
findings.finding: The plan says 'existing boundary controls (gateway / service-mesh mTLS / network policy) that may live outside the diff are considered before BLOCK' but does not specify HOW the detector should handle auth/gateway evidence that is absent from the diff — should it downgrade to WARN automatically, require a specific suppression annotation, or prompt the LLM reviewer? This is a real design decision deferred to the executor.
findings.location: AC3 / FP guards bullet: 'existing boundary controls (gateway / service-mesh mTLS / network policy) that may live outside the diff are considered before BLOCK'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete decision rule such as: 'If boundary controls are not present in the diff, emit severity=HIGH (not BLOCK) with a note that out-of-diff controls were not verified; BLOCK only when the diff itself both opens exposure and removes/lacks auth config.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define, for each non-Terraform file type (Dockerfile, compose, k8s manifests), the concrete token/pattern that constitutes 'auth configured' (e.g., for compose: presence of an auth-proxy service in the same network; for k8s: Ingress with an auth annotation; for Dockerfile: document that auth detection is out of scope and only exposure is flagged).
findings.finding: Auth-absence detection is specified for Terraform (API Gateway authorizer, SG-referencing auth proxy) but left undefined for Dockerfile and compose file types — what token/pattern in a Dockerfile or docker-compose.yml constitutes 'authn config present' is not stated, making those sub-detectors unimplementable without guesswork.
findings.location: AC2: 'a public listener with no authn config / no authenticating gateway is flagged' / Files: Dockerfile / compose

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism for pairing the exposure signal with auth-absence: e.g., two coordinated opengrep rules (one MATCH for exposure, one NOT-MATCH for auth resources) combined in a post-processing step in `detectors.py`, or a taint-mode rule if opengrep supports it for HCL — the plan must describe this join, not just name it as an AC.
findings.finding: The mechanism for pairing the exposure signal with auth-absence is under-specified and may not be achievable with a single opengrep rule. OpenGrep/semgrep rules match a single syntactic pattern per rule; expressing 'SG ingress open to 0.0.0.0/0 AND no authenticating resource (cognito / alb listener rule / API-GW authorizer) present in the same file or module' requires either a multi-rule join in post-processing code or a complex metavariable-pattern that the plan does not specify. Without this, the detector would flag ALL public-exposure patterns regardless of whether auth is present in the same diff, producing the false positives the AC explicitly requires to avoid.
findings.location: AC line 2: 'Pairs the exposure signal with an auth-absence check'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a design note: implement two opengrep rules per exposure class — (1) a positive MATCH rule for the exposure pattern, (2) a NOT-MATCH rule for known auth resources in the same file — and combine their per-file results in a small Python post-processing function in `detectors.py`. For out-of-diff context (gateway config in a separate file), fall back to a severity downgrade rather than a false BLOCK.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the new IaC detectors use the `paths` file-glob envelope field (e.g. `paths: ['**/*.tf']`) rather than a `languages:` declaration, to route correctly through `_is_applicable()` — HCL and YAML are not in `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` and will silently produce `unsupported_lang` abstains.
findings.finding: The existing detector routing in `engine_b._is_applicable()` does not include `hcl` or `terraform` in `_LANG_EXTENSIONS`, so a new detector declaring `languages: [hcl]` will route as `unsupported_lang` and silently abstain on every *.tf diff — meaning the new IaC detectors will never fire under the current engine. The plan targets `*.tf` files but does not address this routing gap.
findings.location: File `src/rebar/grounding/engine_b.py`, `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` dict; AC line 4 ('Fires on the code-review file-glob triggers').
findings.suggested_fix: In each new detector YAML, set `metadata.rebar_envelope.paths: ['**/*.tf']` (or `['**/*.yaml', '**/docker-compose*.yml']` for compose/k8s) instead of `languages: [hcl]`. Verify that `_is_applicable` honors the `paths` glob branch for these file types.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify the criterion assignment and fail_mode for the new public-exposure detectors: either justify filing under existing `high-critical-security` (fail-closed, will BLOCK on abstain) or introduce a new DET criterion (e.g. `iac-public-exposure`) with `fail_mode: open` and `blocking_enabled: false` (advisory until proven) — the current plan is silent on this critical design decision.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the new public-exposure detectors are filed under the existing fail-closed `high-critical-security` criterion or a new advisory DET criterion. Filing under `high-critical-security` means an abstain (tool unavailable, HCL unrecognized) triggers a hard BLOCK on every *.tf-touching PR — an unacceptable blast radius for a new, unproven detector class. A new `fail_mode: open` criterion is the safer fit but the plan is silent on this design choice.
findings.location: AC line 3 ('FP guards: ... Aligns with + cites tfsec / Checkov SG checks'); plan section 'Files: criteria_routing.json (the security / iac / high-critical-security entries)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new `iac-public-exposure` entry to criteria_routing.json with `exec: DET`, `fail_mode: open`, `blocking_enabled: false`, and `applies_to: ['**/*.tf', '**/docker-compose*.yml', '**/k8s/**']`. Promote to fail-closed only after the detector's FP rate is validated in production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the structural contradiction between 'deterministic detector' and 'consider boundary controls outside the diff': either (a) limit the deterministic detector to exposure patterns only and delegate the out-of-diff auth-context check to the AGENT-exec `iac` overlay (which has file-read tools), or (b) explicitly accept that the detector cannot see out-of-diff context and document that out-of-diff boundary controls require a developer annotation or the iac/security LLM overlay to suppress the finding.
findings.finding: The AC requires that 'existing boundary controls (gateway / service-mesh mTLS / network policy) that may live OUTSIDE the diff are considered before BLOCK,' but a deterministic opengrep detector operating on changed files structurally cannot see resources or configurations outside the diff. The plan's mechanism (opengrep diff-scoped rules) is architecturally incapable of satisfying this guard, yet the AC states it as a requirement without specifying a fallback.
findings.location: AC line 3: 'existing boundary controls (gateway / service-mesh mTLS / network policy) that may live outside the diff are considered before BLOCK'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the execution mechanism for the new public-exposure detectors: if they are diff-text regex/pattern matchers (not Engine B gitleaks/opengrep tools), specify whether they run as a new `exec` type, a new scripted step, or a modification to the `iac`/`security` LLM overlay prompts — `detectors.py` as currently architected only orchestrates Engine B tools.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that new public-exposure detectors will live in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py`, but the actual file exclusively orchestrates Engine B tools (gitleaks via SARIF, opengrep YAML rules) through `rebar.grounding.engine_b.scan`. CIDR-regex / bind-address pattern matching is a fundamentally different execution model — it cannot slot into the existing `exec: DET` + `id_prefix` routing without either being registered as a new Engine B detector or requiring a different execution path that the plan does not specify.
findings.location: Files section / `src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether the new public-exposure detectors register as engine_b/opengrep rules (picked up automatically by the DET pipeline) or as inline Python functions — and if inline, add `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` (or the equivalent call site) to the explicit edit set.
findings.finding: The plan is ambiguous about whether the new public-exposure detectors plug into the existing `exec: DET` / `engine_b.scan()` pipeline (which `apply_failclosed` in `gate_dispatch.py` automatically invokes) or are standalone Python regex checks added directly to `detectors.py`. If the latter, `gate_dispatch.py` — which currently calls only `apply_failclosed` — is an unenumerated call-site that would need a second invocation of the new detector function, but is not listed in the plan's edit set.
findings.location: Files section; src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py (new detector description); src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py line 475

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve whether opengrep's single-file AST matching can implement the cross-resource auth-absence check in AC2, or specify an alternative mechanism (e.g., whole-directory scan with `--include`, a post-processing join step, or constraining the check to same-file configurations only).
findings.finding: The plan's auth-absence check ('a public listener with no authn config / no authenticating gateway is flagged') is a cross-resource and cross-file analysis that opengrep — a single-file AST pattern matcher — cannot reliably perform, but this capability limitation is not acknowledged or resolved.
findings.location: AC2 ('Pairs the exposure signal with an auth-absence check')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan whether the new public-exposure detector is a NEW `criteria_routing.json` DET entry or an extension of `high-critical-security`, and state its `fail_mode` (open vs. closed) and `blocking_enabled` posture, with explicit rationale for the blast-radius trade-off.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the new public-exposure-without-auth detector is added as a NEW `exec: "DET"` routing entry in `criteria_routing.json` (with its own `blocking_enabled` and `fail_mode`) or as an extension of the existing `high-critical-security` id_prefix. The existing DET routing seam (registry.py `det_criteria()` / `criterion_for_detector()`) is data-driven from the routing JSON, so this design choice has direct blast-radius consequences: a `fail_mode: "closed"` entry will BLOCK all IaC reviews if the new detector tool is unavailable (abstain → forced BLOCK), while `fail_mode: "open"` will record a coverage gap only.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json (DET entries, lines 80–97) / src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a routing-entry design section: create a new `public-exposure-without-auth` DET entry with `fail_mode: "open"` and `blocking_enabled: false` initially (advisory, matching the `iac` AGENT criterion's posture), escalating to `fail_mode: "closed"` / `blocking_enabled: true` only after detector reliability is established — consistent with the WS5 precedent for `secret-detection`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement the applicability routing for new IaC detectors: either extend `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` in `engine_b.py` with `'hcl'`/`'terraform'`/`'yaml'` entries, or declare the detectors using envelope `paths:` globs (e.g. `paths: ['**/*.tf']`) instead of `languages:`, and confirm the chosen approach covers all target file types (*.tf, Dockerfile, k8s YAML, compose YAML).
findings.finding: The engine_b.py `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` map has no entries for 'hcl', 'terraform', or 'yaml', so a new detector declaring `languages: [hcl]` or `languages: [yaml]` will route as `unsupported_lang` and silently abstain on every scan — causing either a spurious fail-CLOSED BLOCK on every diff (if routed through `high-critical-security`) or silent non-detection. The plan does not address which applicability routing mechanism (languages vs. envelope paths globs) the new IaC detectors will use.
findings.location: src/rebar/grounding/engine_b.py lines 62-72 (_LANG_EXTENSIONS); AC: 'Fires on the code-review file-glob triggers (*.tf / Dockerfile / k8s / compose)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use envelope `paths:` globs in the new detector YAML files (e.g. `rebar_envelope: { paths: ['**/*.tf'] }`) rather than a `languages:` declaration, since `_is_applicable` checks `file_globs` via `repo_root.glob(g)` independently of `_LANG_EXTENSIONS`. This requires no changes to engine_b.py and is consistent with how the gitleaks sentinel (which declares no language) already works.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the specific public-exposure + auth-absence instruction text to both code-review-security.md and code-review-iac.md in the plan (or as a diff snippet): specify the positive rule (flag public listener + no authn), the negative rules (loopback / gateway-fronted / private-CIDR → do not flag or downgrade), and the evidence format (cite the specific resource block / CIDR / port).
findings.finding: The decision rule for the auth-absence paired check is not co-located with the overlay prompts that must execute it. The plan says the security/iac overlay prompts will flag 'a public listener with no authn config / no authenticating gateway' and NOT flag 'loopback-only / gateway-fronted / private-CIDR-sourced services', but code-review-security.md and code-review-iac.md contain no such instruction today and the plan never defines the exact instruction text to add — creating an instruction-locality gap.
findings.location: Plan section 'Files: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/' / AC bullet 2 / src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-security.md / src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-iac.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the canonical `rebar.builtin.security.*` detector id(s) for the new public-exposure-without-auth rule(s) in the plan, and specify whether they route through the existing `high-critical-security` DET criterion or a new dedicated criterion in `criteria_routing.json`.
findings.finding: The new 'publicly-exposed-without-auth' detector ID(s) are never defined. The plan says to add detectors to detectors.py and criteria_routing.json using the rebar.builtin.security.* pattern, but no detector ID string (e.g. rebar.builtin.security.public-exposure-no-auth) is specified anywhere in the plan, leaving the criterion_for_detector() routing and the opengrep/regex rule ID ambiguous.
findings.location: Plan section 'Files: detectors.py' / AC bullet 1-3 / src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json (high-critical-security entry)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as: 'New detector id: `rebar.builtin.security.public-service-no-auth` — routes via the existing `id_prefix: rebar.builtin.security.` selector to `high-critical-security`.' If a separate severity posture is wanted for FP-guarded cases, define a second criterion entry with `blocking_enabled: false`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike/fixture set with ≥3 representative diff samples per file type (TF, k8s, compose) that exercises the auth-absence pairing logic and document the measured FP/FN rate before merging the detector.
findings.finding: The auth-absence heuristic — what constitutes 'authentication configured' across Terraform (aws_alb_listener, aws_api_gateway, aws_cognito_*), k8s Ingress annotations, Docker Compose labels, and Dockerfiles — is asserted as implementable but never empirically probed. The AC unit tests cover only the simplest positive (0.0.0.0/0 + no auth) and negative (loopback/private-CIDR) cases, leaving the FP/FN rate of the pairing logic unvalidated across the target file types.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Pairs the exposure signal with an auth-absence check' + 'Unit tests covering a positive … and a negative'
findings.suggested_fix: Create a `tests/detectors/fixtures/` directory with real-world snippet fixtures (one TF snippet with aws_cognito authorizer, one k8s Ingress with oauth2-proxy annotation, one without) and assert correct PASS/FAIL outcomes in the unit tests, giving empirical coverage of the pairing heuristic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why bespoke opengrep rules are preferred over wiring tfsec or Checkov through the existing `BACKEND_SARIF` sentinel-descriptor pattern (ADR 0012), given they natively handle HCL2/k8s and emit SARIF 2.1.0.
findings.finding: The plan proposes writing bespoke opengrep rules for the same Terraform/k8s exposure checks that tfsec and Checkov already implement natively — but never considers using them via the existing SARIF-ingest backend (BACKEND_SARIF / ADR 0012), which was introduced precisely to absorb external SARIF-emitting scanners without a new backend.
findings.location: Context / Files section; AC3 ('Aligns with + cites tfsec … / Checkov SG checks')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Alternatives considered' section documenting the tfsec/Checkov-via-BACKEND_SARIF option and the specific reason it was rejected (e.g., binary dependency surface, offline/vendored requirement, rule-subset control). If no strong reason exists, adopt the SARIF-ingest approach: add a sentinel descriptor for tfsec (analogous to `security_secrets_gitleaks.yaml`) and route its criterion via `criteria_routing.json` — no new opengrep rules needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the 'LOWER severity, not a hard block' FP-guard maps to the Pass-3 decision schema: e.g., 'private-CIDR findings are emitted under a criterion with `blocking_enabled: false` (advisory only), while 0.0.0.0/0 findings go to `high-critical-security` (blocking_enabled: true)' — and add this mapping to criteria_routing.json.
findings.finding: The FP-guard severity decision rule ('private-CIDR or named-SG ingress is LOWER severity, not a hard block') is stated only in prose AC bullet 3 but is never mapped to the Pass-3 confidence/threshold schema. The plan does not specify whether 'LOWER severity' means a lower numeric confidence score passed to threshold_for(), a different blocking_enabled flag, or a separate criterion — leaving the decision rule unco-located with the threshold schema it must drive.
findings.location: AC bullet 3 / src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py threshold_for() / criteria_routing.json

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the FP-guard severity mapping (private-CIDR → LOWER, named-SG → LOWER, public-IP → BLOCK) into a configurable policy dict or YAML, and add an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the decision to adopt a deterministic tfsec/Checkov-style detector layer within the code-review overlay.
findings.finding: The FP-guard severity rules ('private-CIDR-sourced or named-SG ingress is LOWER severity, not a hard block') and the BLOCK vs. WARN thresholds are policy decisions expected to evolve as edge cases accumulate, but the plan places them in detectors.py with no mention of configurability and cites no ADR or CLAUDE.md update to capture the architectural decision to introduce a new detector pattern.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'FP guards' bullet; Files — 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `detector_policy.yaml` (or a top-of-file `POLICY` dict in detectors.py) that maps exposure-type → default severity, and add one paragraph to CLAUDE.md (or a new `docs/adr/NNN-deterministic-exposure-detectors.md`) explaining the detector contract and how to extend it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate which existing reviewers/pipeline steps consume the `security`, `iac`, and `high-critical-security` routing entries, confirm the planned changes are purely additive (new detector refs only), and explicitly state any changed entry semantics so downstream consumers can be validated.
findings.finding: The plan modifies existing `criteria_routing.json` entries (`security`, `iac`, `high-critical-security`) but does not acknowledge or enumerate downstream consumers of the current routing behavior that may be affected by those changes.
findings.location: Files section / criteria_routing.json (`security` / `iac` / `high-critical-security` entries)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Routing config impact' subsection that lists: (a) current consumers of the three routing keys, (b) whether each entry change is additive-only or modifies existing match criteria/severity thresholds, and (c) confirmation that existing detectors remain unaffected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml` to the plan's explicit edit set (or confirm no new criterion/prompt ID is introduced, so the YAML needs no change).
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml` is an unenumerated consumer: it hard-codes `when:` step entries for each reviewer prompt and for the `include_*` flags derived from `criteria_routing.json`. If the plan adds a new `criteria_routing.json` criterion or a new reviewer prompt file, this YAML requires a matching new step — but it is not listed in the plan's edit set.
findings.location: Files section (plan names criteria_routing.json, detectors.py, reviewers prompt — does not name code-review.yaml); src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml lines 72, 80, 100, 108

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit tests asserting FP-guard behavior: (a) private-CIDR-sourced ingress yields LOWER severity, not BLOCK; (b) a public listener paired with an authenticating gateway/mTLS annotation in the same diff is NOT flagged; (c) a named-SG source is not treated as BLOCK.
findings.finding: The testing AC has no negative test for the auth-absence-pairing / FP-guard logic: a public listener WITH an authenticating gateway or service-mesh mTLS in the same diff should NOT be flagged, and a private-CIDR-sourced ingress should produce LOWER severity — neither invariant has a named test case.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — FP guards bullet and last (unit-tests) bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `detectors.py` requires NO modification for this feature — the new public-exposure detector should be implemented as a YAML file under `src/rebar/grounding/detectors/builtin/` + a new `exec: "DET"` entry in `criteria_routing.json`, following the established data-driven seam (story 7f0d).
findings.finding: The plan lists `src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py` as a file to modify, but since story 7f0d the consumer is fully data-driven — new detector types are registered via a YAML file in `grounding/detectors/builtin/` and a new `exec: "DET"` entry in `criteria_routing.json`, with zero changes to `detectors.py`. Modifying that file for this feature would be unnecessary code churn (YAGNI).
findings.location: Plan section 'Files': `src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py` from the file-impact list. Add instead: (1) `src/rebar/grounding/detectors/builtin/security_iac_public_exposure.yaml` (the opengrep/HCL rules for 0.0.0.0/0 SG ingress, public IP association, non-loopback bind), and (2) a new `"public-exposure-without-auth"` entry in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json` with `exec: "DET"`, `detector: {"id_prefix": "rebar.builtin.security.iac."}`, `fail_mode`, and `blocking_enabled`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `**/docker-compose*.yml` and `**/compose*.yaml` globs to the `iac` criterion's `applies_to` in `criteria_routing.json`, or explicitly narrow the AC to exclude compose if that scope is intentional.
findings.finding: The plan's AC states the detector 'Fires on the code-review file-glob triggers (`*.tf` / Dockerfile / k8s / compose)', but `compose` / `docker-compose.yml` is absent from the `iac` criterion's `applies_to` globs in the actual `criteria_routing.json`. A diff touching only `docker-compose.yml` would not trigger the `iac` overlay under the current routing.
findings.location: AC line 4 / `src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json` line 61
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"**/docker-compose*.yml"`, `"**/compose*.yaml"` to the `iac` `applies_to` array in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add compose file globs (`**/compose.yml`, `**/compose.yaml`, `**/docker-compose.yml`, `**/docker-compose.yaml`) to the `iac` criterion's `applies_to` list in `criteria_routing.json`, or explicitly note that compose is out of scope for v1.
findings.finding: The plan's AC requires the detector to fire on `compose` file diffs, but the `iac` criterion's `applies_to` globs in `criteria_routing.json` contain no compose glob (`**/compose.yml`, `**/docker-compose.yml`, `**/compose.yaml`), and the plan does not list adding one as an explicit edit — making the compose trigger AC unachievable without an unenumerated edit to the routing JSON.
findings.location: AC line: 'Fires on the code-review file-glob triggers (*.tf / Dockerfile / k8s / compose)' vs. src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json → iac.applies_to
findings.suggested_fix: In criteria_routing.json, extend the `iac` entry's `applies_to` array with `"**/docker-compose.yml"`, `"**/docker-compose.yaml"`, `"**/compose.yml"`, `"**/compose.yaml"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add compose file globs (e.g. `**/docker-compose*.yml`, `**/docker-compose*.yaml`, `**/compose*.yaml`) to the `iac` `applies_to` list in `criteria_routing.json` (or to the new DET criterion's entry), and ensure the new detector's scanner supports compose file syntax.
findings.finding: The plan's AC explicitly names `compose` (Docker Compose) files as a trigger target, but the existing `iac` glob in `criteria_routing.json` does not include `docker-compose*.yaml`, `compose*.yaml`, or `**/compose/**` patterns, and the plan does not specify what glob(s) to add.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (AC line 4) / src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json line 61
findings.suggested_fix: In `criteria_routing.json` under `iac.applies_to`, append: `"**/docker-compose*.yml"`, `"**/docker-compose*.yaml"`, `"**/compose*.yaml"`. Mirror the same globs in any new DET routing entry for the public-exposure detector.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add compose file globs (`**/compose.yaml`, `**/docker-compose*.yml`, `**/compose*.yaml`) to the `iac` entry's `applies_to` list in `criteria_routing.json` (the plan must specify this change, not just reference compose as a target file type in prose).
findings.finding: The `iac` criteria_routing.json `applies_to` glob list omits docker-compose file patterns, but AC bullet 4 requires the detector to fire on 'compose' diffs. The existing glob list covers *.tf, *.tfvars, k8s/**, helm/**, ansible/**, Dockerfile* — compose.yaml / docker-compose*.yml are not listed, so a diff touching only a compose file will not trigger the iac overlay.
findings.location: AC bullet 4 / src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json (iac entry applies_to)
findings.suggested_fix: In criteria_routing.json, extend iac.applies_to to include: "**/docker-compose*.yml", "**/docker-compose*.yaml", "**/compose.yaml", "**/compose*.yml".

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC5 to require at least one positive unit-test fixture for each distinct exposure pattern named in AC1 (SG ingress 0.0.0.0/0, non-loopback bind, public LB/ingress, associate_public_ip_address=true, public API-Gateway route) plus the existing negative case.
findings.finding: The unit-test acceptance criterion (AC5) mandates only one positive case (0.0.0.0/0 + no auth) and one negative case (loopback/private-CIDR), but AC1 lists at least five distinct exposure patterns (SG/firewall ingress, non-loopback bind, public LB, associate_public_ip_address=true, public API-Gateway route). The other four patterns have no required test, so the test suite could be green while those detectors are absent or broken.
findings.location: AC5: 'Unit tests covering a positive (0.0.0.0/0 + no auth) and a negative (loopback / private-CIDR)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Unit tests covering a positive (0.0.0.0/0 + no auth) and a negative (loopback / private-CIDR)' with 'Unit tests covering one positive fixture per AC1 exposure type and at least one negative fixture (loopback, private-CIDR, gateway-fronted); `make lint` green.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit tests for all three exposure-detection branches: (1) non-loopback bind address (positive: `0.0.0.0`/`::`, negative: `127.0.0.1`/unix socket), and (2) public LB / ingress / `associate_public_ip_address=true` / public API-GW (positive + negative for private/internal variant).
findings.finding: The testing AC names only one positive test (SG ingress `0.0.0.0/0` + no auth) and one negative test (loopback/private-CIDR), leaving the bind-address detector branch and the public-LB/ingress/API-GW/EIP detector branch with no specified test cases — two of the three new user-facing detector paths are untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last bullet: 'Unit tests covering a positive (0.0.0.0/0 + no auth) and a negative (loopback / private-CIDR)'
findings.suggested_fix: Expand the final AC to: 'Unit tests covering a positive and negative for each detection branch: (a) SG ingress `0.0.0.0/0`/`::/0` with and without auth; (b) non-loopback bind `0.0.0.0`/`::` vs `127.0.0.1`/unix socket; (c) public LB/ingress/`associate_public_ip_address=true` vs private/internal LB; `make lint` green.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC4: an integration test or CLI invocation that feeds a synthetic *.tf diff (containing 0.0.0.0/0 ingress) through the code-review gate and asserts a finding is returned — not just a unit test of the detector function in isolation.
findings.finding: AC4 ('Fires on the code-review file-glob triggers') has no concrete proving command or integration test that exercises the routing layer (criteria_routing.json → detector dispatch → finding emitted on a real diff). Only `make lint` and unit tests are mentioned, which test detector logic in isolation but cannot prove the routing wiring is correct.
findings.location: AC4: 'Fires on the code-review file-glob triggers (*.tf / Dockerfile / k8s / compose), NOT on plan prose.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or expand AC4/AC5) that explicitly covers the security reviewer prompt update — stating what the prompt must now instruct the LLM to do with deterministic findings and how it will be verified.
findings.finding: The security reviewer prompt under `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` is listed as a plan deliverable in the Files section but no acceptance criterion covers it — there is no AC that verifies the prompt was updated or that specifies what the update must achieve.
findings.location: Files section ('the code-review security reviewer prompt under src/rebar/llm/reviewers/') vs Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC5)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'The security reviewer prompt is updated to instruct the LLM to treat deterministic public-exposure-without-auth findings as high-severity and to surface them verbatim in its output; verified by the existing prompt-snapshot test or a new prompt-content assertion.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC4 (or add a new AC) to explicitly require that `criteria_routing.json` contains the correct file-glob entries (*.tf, Dockerfile, k8s, compose) routing to the new detectors under the security/iac/high-critical-security keys.
findings.finding: `criteria_routing.json` changes (the security/iac/high-critical-security entries) are listed as a deliverable but AC4 ('Fires on the code-review file-glob triggers') only indirectly implies the routing config must be updated — it does not name the file or specify which entries must change.
findings.location: Files section ('src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json … the security / iac / high-critical-security entries') vs AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4: '…verified by a test or config assertion that criteria_routing.json routes *.tf / Dockerfile / docker-compose*.yml / k8s/**/*.yaml to the new public-exposure-without-auth detectors under the security and high-critical-security keys.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note: implement and locally validate the deterministic detector (detectors.py) in isolation first (with a simple CLI harness or unit test), then wire criteria_routing.json, then update the reviewer prompt — each step independently verifiable before the next.
findings.finding: The plan touches 3 files across 3 layers (config routing, detector logic, LLM prompt) with no explicit thin vertical-slice or evidence-gated MVP checkpoint, meaning all three layers must be correct simultaneously before any incremental validation is possible.
findings.location: Plan body — Files section and overall structure

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that ticket a278 exists and that its scope is indeed the plan-level `endpoint_access_contract` T10 criterion; link it explicitly (ticket ID, ADR, or PR) so the scope boundary between plan-level and code-level detection is auditable.
findings.finding: The plan references a predecessor ticket 'a278' as context ('Follow-up to a278') and claims a278 introduced T10's `endpoint_access_contract` at the plan-review level. No file, comment, or reference to 'a278' exists anywhere in the codebase, making this causal premise unverifiable.
findings.location: Context section: 'Follow-up to a278'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
